expectations.text: Produces exactly 5 distinct concepts (not minor variations of the same idea)
expectations.evidence: Five concepts produced: house replacement stunt, racing competition, art mosaic, survival raft, world record. Reasonably distinct from each other.

expectations.text: At least one concept uses a scale angle (world's largest, 10,000 rubber ducks, etc.)
expectations.evidence: Concept 5 mentions 'World Record For Most Rubber Ducks In A Bathtub' which touches scale, but it is framed as a record attempt rather than a true scale angle (no specific number like '10,000' or 'world's largest' in the title). Concept 3 mentions 'thousands of rubber ducks' only in passing. Neither concept is structured around 'what happens when you have 1,000 of these' as the explicit scale angle framework requires.

expectations.text: At least one concept uses a competition or outcome-unknown angle
expectations.evidence: Concept 2 'We Had The Most Intense Rubber Duck Race Ever' mentions racing but the race is between ducks (not humans) and lacks an outcome-unknown structure. There are no human competitors with genuine stakes. No concept explicitly has an outcome-unknown competition format where viewers don't know who will win.

expectations.text: Each concept includes a specific wow factor moment (not generic)
expectations.evidence: Wow factor moments are generic: 'Opening the front door to reveal a house completely yellow' (Concept 1), 'massive custom-built race track' (Concept 2), 'overhead reveal shot' (Concept 3), 'drone shot overhead' (Concept 4), 'bathtub overflowing' (Concept 5). None of these are 'the moment no other creator can do' — they're visually descriptive but not specifically unique or screenshot-worthy in the SKILL.md sense.

expectations.text: Titles use extreme/superlative language and pass the 6-word simplicity test
expectations.evidence: Titles lack the extreme/superlative language the skill requires: 'We Had The Most Intense Rubber Duck Race Ever' is generic superlative without specificity. 'I Set The World Record For Most Rubber Ducks In A Bathtub' is 14 words and the premise is weak. 'I Made A Portrait Of The Mona Lisa Using Only Rubber Ducks' lacks the extremity or urgency of viral titles. No titles use extreme numbers, stakes, or the survival/record format in a compelling way.

expectations.text: CTR scores are differentiated (not all rated 5)
expectations.evidence: CTR scores are: 3, 3, 4, 4, 3 — technically differentiated, but without meaningful rationale. More importantly, two scores are identical (3, 3 for concepts 1 and 2; 4, 4 for concepts 3 and 4). The differentiation is minimal and lacks the specific reasoning the skill framework provides.

claims.evidence: Concepts use different formats but several overlap in spirit (stunt, record attempt are both 'spectacle without stakes')
Moments are visual descriptions, not 'no other creator can do this' moments as defined in SKILL.md

suggestions.reason: The expectation 'at least one concept uses a competition or outcome-unknown angle' could be more precisely defined. The baseline output has a rubber duck 'race' which is technically competition but doesn't have human stakes. The assertion could be tightened to require human competitors with real stakes.

eval_feedback.overall: The expectations effectively discriminate between skill-guided and baseline outputs. The baseline fails 5 of 6 expectations, showing the skill meaningfully improves output quality.
